description:
Lisbon portugal travel tips i wish i knew sooner !! 🪩🏰 hopefully these 13 tips help you on your next trip to Lisbon 🇵🇹 🧐 if you’ve been to lisbon…what am i missing here? drop ur lisbon travel tips!!!  #lisbonportugal #traveltips #lisbontravel #portugaltravel #lisbon 
Practical Lisbon travel tips to save money, skip lines, and enjoy the city — 13 essential Lisbon facts and hacks for first-timers. #lisbonportugal #traveltips #lisbontravel
Lead
Planning a trip to Lisbon? These 13 Lisbon travel tips collect practical, on-the-ground advice to help you save money, move around easily, and enjoy the city like a seasoned visitor. Use them to streamline your packing, sightseeing, eating, and budgeting so your next trip feels smoother and more relaxed.

1. Get a rechargeable transport card
Buy a reloadable Viva Viagem or 24-hour ticket for trams, buses, and metro. It’s cheaper than single fares and saves time at stations. Keep it topped up for airport connections and popular tram routes.

2. Expect hills and pack comfortable shoes
Lisbon’s cobbled streets and steep hills are iconic — bring supportive walking shoes or sneakers. Comfortable footwear makes exploring neighborhoods like Alfama and Bairro Alto far more enjoyable.

3. Mind the cobbles and dress accordingly
Historic areas have uneven pavement; consider a low-heeled, sturdy shoe rather than delicate sandals. A small daypack helps keep belongings secure when navigating narrow streets.

4. Watch your budget in tourist areas
Restaurants and shops near major attractions can be pricier. Venture to side streets or residential neighborhoods for better value and more authentic food.

5. Try pastéis de nata early in the day
These custard tarts are best fresh from the oven. Visit reputable bakeries in the morning to avoid long lines and ensure the flaky texture and warm custard.

6. Learn basic Portuguese greetings
Simple words like “Olá,” “Bom dia,” and “Obrigado/Obrigada” go a long way with locals. English is widely spoken in tourist zones, but small courtesies are appreciated.

7. Use tram 28 smartly — expect crowds
Tram 28 is iconic for a scenic ride through historic districts, but it attracts tourists. Ride early or late to avoid peak crowds and watch your belongings on busy stretches.

8. Plan time for viewpoints (miradouros)
Lisbon’s miradouros offer stellar city and river views. Set aside time for at least one viewpoint like Miradouro da Senhora do Monte or Miradouro de Santa Catarina, ideally at sunset.

9. Book popular sites in advance
For attractions and tours that limit capacity, reserve tickets ahead to avoid sold-out times — this is especially helpful during high season.

10. Consider day trips from Lisbon
Places like Sintra and Cascais are easy day trips by train. Build one or two day excursions into your itinerary for palaces, coastline, and different scenery.

11. Carry a reusable water bottle
Tap water in Lisbon is safe to drink. A reusable bottle saves money and reduces plastic waste while you walk between sights.

12. Be mindful of pickpockets in crowded areas
Busy trams, tourist spots, and markets are where pickpockets operate. Keep valuables secure and stay aware in packed areas.

13. Embrace local shopping and nightlife
Explore local shops away from main avenues for unique souvenirs and more reasonable prices. Bairro Alto and Cais do Sodré offer lively nightlife — check opening times and local recommendations.

Close / Call to action
Which Lisbon tips would you add? Share your best Lisbon travel tips and favorite neighborhoods to help future travelers plan a smarter, more enjoyable trip.

description:
3 days in Lisbon itinerary #lisbon #solofemaletraveler 
Explore Lisbon in 3 days with this solo female traveler itinerary. Discover unique places to eat and top things to do in Lisbon.
Planning a trip to Lisbon? This 3-day itinerary is perfect for solo female travelers looking to experience the best of the city. Discover unique culinary gems and uncover must-do activities that will make your Lisbon adventure unforgettable.

**Day 1: Historic Charm and Bairro Alto Nights**
Start your Lisbon exploration in the historic Alfama district. Wander through its narrow, winding streets, listen to Fado music, and visit the iconic São Jorge Castle for panoramic city views. For lunch, seek out a traditional tasca for some authentic Portuguese cuisine. In the afternoon, take Tram 28 for a scenic ride through the city's most picturesque neighborhoods. As evening approaches, head to the Bairro Alto, known for its vibrant nightlife and diverse dining options. Enjoy dinner at one of the many unique eateries before experiencing the lively atmosphere of its bars.

**Day 2: Belém's Discoveries and Riverside Views**
Dedicate your second day to the historic district of Belém. Marvel at the Jerónimos Monastery and the Belém Tower, both UNESCO World Heritage sites that speak volumes of Portugal's Age of Discovery. Don't miss trying the famous Pastéis de Belém from the original bakery. In the afternoon, enjoy a leisurely walk along the Tagus River or visit the Monument to the Discoveries. Consider a sunset cruise for breathtaking views of the city from the water.

**Day 3: Modern Lisbon and Day Trip Options**
Explore the modern side of Lisbon in the Parque das Nações, built for Expo '98. Visit the Oceanário de Lisboa, one of Europe's largest aquariums, or take a cable car ride for stunning river views. Alternatively, if you're looking for a change of scenery, consider a day trip. Sintra, with its romantic palaces and mystical forests, is a popular choice. Cascais, a charming seaside town, offers a more relaxed coastal experience. Whichever you choose, ensure you allow ample time to soak in the distinct atmosphere of these nearby destinations before returning to Lisbon for your final evening.

Visiting Lisbon for the first time and want to know what NOT to see? I’d probably avoid these five things as a local.  Everyone has their own preferences and I’m not saying to *not* visit them per se, and I would never say not to patronize a business. But in the interest of time and other sites … I’d deprioritize these choices. See the full video for why. 1) Time Out Market 2) Pink Street 3) Lisbon Cathedral 4) Santa Justa Lift 5) Tram 28 #lisboa #lisbon #blackqueer #travel 
Visiting Lisbon for the first time? Discover 5 popular tourist spots a local would deprioritize to save time and find more authentic experiences. #lisboa #lisbon #travel
Planning your first trip to Lisbon and wondering what to skip? As a local with three years of experience in the city, Keith shares five popular tourist attractions that might not be worth your time, especially if you're looking for authentic Portuguese experiences and are short on time.

First on the list is the **Time Out Market**, located in the historic Mercado da Ribeira. While it's a good meeting point, Keith describes it as a glorified food court, especially crowded on weekends with limited authentic Portuguese restaurant options. Finding a table can be a challenge, making it difficult for families or groups seeking genuine cuisine.

Next, **Pink Street** is highlighted as an area solely catered towards tourists, focusing on pub crawls and shots. While the area has been cleaned up from its sketchier past, Keith personally avoids it and mentions a friend who was robbed there. For a better nightlife experience, he suggests alternative areas like Bairro Alto or Prince of Beirut, though he notes the amazing bar Pensao Amor is located there.

Surprisingly, the **Lisbon Cathedral**, the city's oldest, built in 1147, is also recommended for deprioritization. Despite its historical significance and survival through the 18th-century earthquake, the interior is described as underwhelming, with a seven-euro fee for tours and frequent closures for services. Keith suggests exploring other historic religious sites in Lisbon or simply popping in and out without paying the fee.

The **Santa Justa Lift** (Elevador de Santa Justa) is another point of contention. Keith expresses bewilderment at its appeal, noting the hour-long queues for a city view that can be enjoyed for free from numerous 'miradouros' (viewpoints) scattered throughout Lisbon. He has never ridden it himself, finding the appeal of just an elevator to be inexplicable.

Finally, the controversial **Tram 28** makes the list. While it offers scenic views of Alfama and the city center, it's notoriously overcrowded, especially in summer, and lacks air conditioning, making the ride uncomfortable. The old tram's bumpy journey is not smooth. Keith suggests riding it for a few stops, preferably downhill from Alfama, or opting for other trams that provide a similar experience without the crowds and discomfort.

Keith emphasizes that these are suggestions for those with limited time or seeking more original Lisbon experiences. He never advises against visiting places or patronizing businesses, acknowledging that everyone has different travel preferences. For more insider tips on Lisbon, follow for practical advice.

description:
🇵🇹👇 This list is for you if you're visiting Lisbon for the first time: ✨ Praça do Comércio – Start your day at Lisbon’s most iconic (and touristic) square by the river. ✨ Lisbon Cathedral – Walk uphill to the oldest cathedral in the city, also known as Sé de Lisboa. ✨ Miradouro de Santa Luzia – One of the most beautiful viewpoints, with azulejos (tiles) and views over the Alfama neighborhood. ✨ History of Lisbon Mural by Nuno Saraiva – A colorful mural that tells Lisbon’s history through illustrations. ✨ Explore Alfama – Walk down the stairs and get lost in Lisbon’s oldest neighborhood. ✨ Casa São Miguel cafe– A charming bakery in Alfama famous for its traditional Portuguese pastries ✨ Ginjinha da Dona Alice – Stop for a quick shot of Lisbon’s famous cherry liqueur. ✨ Taberna Sal Grosso – Perfect spot for a  Portuguese lunch (book in advance). ✨ National Pantheon – An iconic monument with panoramic views from the terrace. ✨ Feira da Ladra – Lisbon’s famous flea market (Tuesdays & Saturdays). ✨ Igreja de São Vicente de Fora – A stunning church with one of the best rooftop views in Lisbon. ✨ Miradouro da Graça – A popular viewpoint with beautiful city and river views, perfect for photos or a quick coffee outside. #lisbontravel #lisbonportugal #lisboaportugal 
Discover Lisbon's must-see spots for first-time travelers! From iconic squares to delightful pastries, explore the best of Portugal's capital. #lisbontravel #lisbonportugal #lisboaportugal
Planning your first trip to Lisbon? This beautiful city offers a wealth of attractions, perfect for first-time visitors. Start your adventure at Praça do Comércio, Lisbon’s most iconic square, where you can soak up the vibrant atmosphere by the river. From here, take a stroll to the Lisbon Cathedral, known as Sé de Lisboa, the oldest cathedral in the city that showcases stunning architecture and historical significance.

Don’t miss out on the Miradouro de Santa Luzia, one of the most picturesque viewpoints in Lisbon. Adorned with beautiful azulejos (traditional tiles), it provides breathtaking views of the Alfama neighborhood. While in the area, take note of the History of Lisbon Mural by Nuno Saraiva. This colorful work of art narrates the story of Lisbon through engaging illustrations.

Exploring Alfama is an essential experience; wander through its narrow streets, and embrace the charm of the city’s oldest neighborhood. Make sure to stop by Casa São Miguel cafe for traditional Portuguese pastries, a perfect treat during your adventure.

For a taste of local culture, try Ginjinha da Dona Alice, renowned for its delightful cherry liqueur. For lunch, Taberna Sal Grosso offers a delightful selection of Portuguese dishes—just be sure to book in advance to secure your spot.

Continue your exploration at the National Pantheon, an iconic monument that provides panoramic views of the city from its terrace, an experience not to be missed. If you love shopping for unique finds, the Feira da Ladra, Lisbon’s famous flea market, is open on Tuesdays and Saturdays, making it a great place to pick up souvenirs.

Lastly, visit Igreja de São Vicente de Fora, a stunning church that boasts one of the best rooftop views of Lisbon. For another great vantage point, head to the Miradouro da Graça, popular for its beautiful city and river views. Whether you are taking photos or enjoying a coffee, this viewpoint is a lovely spot to unwind.

These attractions are just a glimpse of what Lisbon has to offer. Be sure to immerse yourself in the culture, enjoy local delicacies, and embrace the vibrant atmosphere of Portugal’s captivating capital.

Explore Lisbon in one day with this complete itinerary! Visit iconic spots, enjoy local cuisine, and soak in stunning views. #lisbonitinerary #visitlisbon #portugaltravel
### A Perfect One-Day Itinerary in Lisbon 🇵🇹

If you're planning to spend just one day in the beautiful city of Lisbon, you're in for a treat! While I always recommend taking more time to explore, Lisbon is quite easy to navigate, making it possible to pack a full day of adventure into your itinerary. Here’s how to make the most of your day in this stunning city.

**Morning:**  
Start your day in **Alfama**, the oldest neighborhood in Lisbon, and if you're visiting on a Tuesday or Saturday, don't miss the vibrant flea market! After exploring, take a leisurely stroll to **Miradouro das Portas do Sol** and **Miradouro Santa Luzia**. These viewpoints offer breathtaking views over the city and are just a short walk from one another. ☀️

Next, grab a coffee at **Café de Garagem**, known for its incredible vistas. It's the perfect way to energize before continuing your adventure. Then, hop onto the iconic **Tram 28**. Riding one of these historic yellow trams is a must when in Lisbon. You can stay on for as long or as little as you wish, allowing for flexibility in your plans.

**Midday:**  
Disembark at **Praça do Comércio**, a grand plaza that overlooks the Tagus River. From there, make your way to **Pink Street**, a lively area filled with vibrant bars and restaurants. Don't forget to stop by the **Time Out Market** to indulge in a delicious **pastel de nata** – a quintessential Portuguese treat! 🥐

### A Perfect One-Day Itinerary in Lisbon 🇵🇹

If you're planning to spend just one day in the beautiful city of Lisbon, you're in for a treat! While I always recommend taking more time to explore, Lisbon is quite easy to navigate, making it possible to pack a full day of adventure into your itinerary. Here’s how to make the most of your day in this stunning city.

**Morning:**  
Start your day in **Alfama**, the oldest neighborhood in Lisbon, and if you're visiting on a Tuesday or Saturday, don't miss the vibrant flea market! After exploring, take a leisurely stroll to **Miradouro das Portas do Sol** and **Miradouro Santa Luzia**. These viewpoints offer breathtaking views over the city and are just a short walk from one another. ☀️

Next, grab a coffee at **Café de Garagem**, known for its incredible vistas. It's the perfect way to energize before continuing your adventure. Then, hop onto the iconic **Tram 28**. Riding one of these historic yellow trams is a must when in Lisbon. You can stay on for as long or as little as you wish, allowing for flexibility in your plans.

**Midday:**  
Disembark at **Praça do Comércio**, a grand plaza that overlooks the Tagus River. From there, make your way to **Pink Street**, a lively area filled with vibrant bars and restaurants. Don't forget to stop by the **Time Out Market** to indulge in a delicious **pastel de nata** – a quintessential Portuguese treat! 🥐

For lunch, enjoy a meal with a view at **Java Rooftop**, where you'll savor delicious cuisine surrounded by the cityscape. Afterward, take the **Bica Funicular** to reach the trendy **Bairro Alto** neighborhood.

**Afternoon:**  
From Bairro Alto, take a taxi to **Belém**, known for its historical significance and monuments. A quick taxi ride will also lead you to the **LX Factory**, a unique cultural space filled with eclectic shops, restaurants, and creative spaces. It’s the perfect spot to explore and perhaps pick up a souvenir! 🎨

**Evening:**  
As the day winds down, head over to **Jardim do Duque** for a spectacular sunset view. This beautiful garden provides a serene atmosphere for relaxation. Finally, treat yourself to dinner at **Ponto Final**, renowned for its stunning views and delectable dishes. 🌅

With this itinerary, you can experience the essence of Lisbon in just one day! For more inspiration and details, check out my blog.

description:
Fun things to do in Lisbon this summer ✨  #lisbon #lisboa #lisbontravel #portugal🇵🇹 #lisbonguide
Discover top summer activities in Lisbon: sightseeing, beaches, food, and nightlife—practical ideas for an unforgettable Lisbon trip. #lisbon #lisboa #lisbontravel #portugal🇵🇹 #lisbonguide
Lead
Lisbon in summer is lively, sunlit and full of easy-to-follow experiences: city viewpoints, historic neighborhoods, riverside sunsets, coastal beaches, Portuguese food, and vibrant nightlife. This guide lists practical, seasonal ideas to help you plan memorable days in Lisbon.

Top sights and neighborhoods
- Alfama: Walk its narrow streets to feel Lisbon’s history. Visit viewpoints (miradouros) for classic city and river panoramas. Expect steep cobbled lanes and local fado houses.
- Baixa and Chiado: Central squares, cafés, bakeries and shopping streets. Ideal for daytime strolling and sampling pastéis de nata.
- Bairro Alto: Daytime quiet turns into lively nightlife after dark—bars and small music venues cluster around its streets.
- Belém: Riverside monuments and pastries. See Jerónimos Monastery, Belém Tower and try the iconic pastéis de nata at a nearby bakery.

Sun, river and beaches
- Tagus riverside: Walk or cycle along the river for relaxed evening sunsets and outdoor cafés. The riverfront atmosphere is ideal after sightseeing.
- Day-trip beaches: Short trips from Lisbon take you to Cascais and Estoril for sandy beaches, seaside promenades and surfing options. For calmer coves, head to smaller coastal spots west of Cascais.

Food, cafés and markets
- Pastéis de nata: Try them fresh at a traditional pastry shop in Belém or downtown cafés.
- Seafood and petiscos: Sample grilled fish, octopus, and small plates (petiscos) in local tascas and seaside restaurants.
- Markets: Explore food markets and fresh-produce stalls to taste local cheeses, olives and seasonal fruit.

Views, photo spots and sunsets
- Miradouros (viewpoints): Visit multiple viewpoints across the city for sweeping vistas—perfect for golden-hour photos.
- Rooftop bars: Enjoy cocktails with city views in Bairro Alto and central Lisbon rooftop venues.

Culture and experiences
- Fado: Attend an intimate fado performance in Alfama or a traditional house to hear Portugal’s melancholy song style.
- Museums and monuments: Include short visits to Jerónimos Monastery, Belém Tower and selected museums to balance outdoor exploring with cultural stops.

Practical tips for summer in Lisbon
- Sun and comfort: Summer sun is strong—carry sunscreen, a hat and water for daytime walks through hills and cobbled streets.
- Footwear: Wear comfortable shoes for steep climbs and uneven pavement.
- Transport: Use trams, metro and ferries to save time. Day trips to nearby beaches are simple by train.
- Timing: Start early for popular sights to avoid midday heat and crowds; enjoy riverside evenings for cooler temperatures and sunset views.

Closing / Call-to-action
Plan a mix of city exploration, riverbank relaxation and short beach trips to make the most of Lisbon this summer. Which part of Lisbon are you most excited to see—historic Alfama, riverside Belém, or the nearby beaches?

Lisbon, the vibrant capital of Portugal, offers a wealth of experiences for travelers. Spending a full day in this stunning city can reveal historical landmarks, delicious cuisine, and a lively atmosphere. Start your day at the iconic Belém Tower, a UNESCO World Heritage site that provides insights into Portugal's maritime history. Enjoy a pastel de nata, a traditional Portuguese custard tart, from the renowned Pastéis de Belém bakery nearby.

Next, make your way to the historic Alfama district, where narrow streets wind through centuries-old buildings. Here, you can visit the impressive São Jorge Castle, which boasts panoramic views of the city. Following your castle exploration, immerse yourself in the local culture by attending a Fado music performance, a traditional genre celebrating Portuguese soulful ballads.

For lunch, head to the Time Out Market, where you can sample a variety of Portuguese dishes from top chefs. A must-try is Bacalhau à Brás, a delectable cod dish. Afterward, consider strolling along the Praça do Comércio, a large waterfront square perfect for relaxing and taking in the scenic views.

As the day winds down, catch the sunset at Miradouro da Senhora do Monte, one of the best viewpoints in the city. This picturesque spot offers one of the most beautiful views of Lisbon as the sun dips below the horizon.

Wrap up your day with dinner in the Bairro Alto district, known for its nightlife and trendy restaurants. Whether you’re enjoying local seafood or sampling fine wines, the culinary delights of Lisbon will leave you wanting more.

Lisbon is enchanting year-round, making it a perfect destination for a memorable girl trip or a solo adventure. If you have any questions about your visit to this incredible city, feel free to ask!

Discover Lisbon's must-see spots for first-time travelers! From iconic squares to delightful pastries, explore the best of Portugal's capital. #lisbontravel #lisbonportugal #lisboaportugal
Planning your first trip to Lisbon? This beautiful city offers a wealth of attractions, perfect for first-time visitors. Start your adventure at Praça do Comércio, Lisbon’s most iconic square, where you can soak up the vibrant atmosphere by the river. From here, take a stroll to the Lisbon Cathedral, known as Sé de Lisboa, the oldest cathedral in the city that showcases stunning architecture and historical significance.

Don’t miss out on the Miradouro de Santa Luzia, one of the most picturesque viewpoints in Lisbon. Adorned with beautiful azulejos (traditional tiles), it provides breathtaking views of the Alfama neighborhood. While in the area, take note of the History of Lisbon Mural by Nuno Saraiva. This colorful work of art narrates the story of Lisbon through engaging illustrations.

Exploring Alfama is an essential experience; wander through its narrow streets, and embrace the charm of the city’s oldest neighborhood. Make sure to stop by Casa São Miguel cafe for traditional Portuguese pastries, a perfect treat during your adventure.

For a taste of local culture, try Ginjinha da Dona Alice, renowned for its delightful cherry liqueur. For lunch, Taberna Sal Grosso offers a delightful selection of Portuguese dishes—just be sure to book in advance to secure your spot.

Continue your exploration at the National Pantheon, an iconic monument that provides panoramic views of the city from its terrace, an experience not to be missed. If you love shopping for unique finds, the Feira da Ladra, Lisbon’s famous flea market, is open on Tuesdays and Saturdays, making it a great place to pick up souvenirs.

Lastly, visit Igreja de São Vicente de Fora, a stunning church that boasts one of the best rooftop views of Lisbon. For another great vantage point, head to the Miradouro da Graça, popular for its beautiful city and river views. Whether you are taking photos or enjoying a coffee, this viewpoint is a lovely spot to unwind.

These attractions are just a glimpse of what Lisbon has to offer. Be sure to immerse yourself in the culture, enjoy local delicacies, and embrace the vibrant atmosphere of Portugal’s captivating capital.

Guide for POC travellers: top Portugal spots—Lisbon, Porto, and the south coast—what to expect and why they’re most recommended. #blackgirl #holidays #portugal #poc
Lead
Most POC travellers visiting Portugal single out three go-to areas: Lisbon, Porto and the south coast. These spots consistently come up in recommendations for atmosphere, accessibility and welcoming vibes.

What POC travellers report about each place
- Lisbon: The capital is the most commonly chosen destination. It offers diverse neighbourhoods, a vibrant cultural scene, frequent international flights, and visible communities that make navigating city life easier. Expect plentiful cafés, museums, nightlife and easy public transport.

- Porto: Portugal’s second city is the next most-ordered pick. Porto combines historic streets, riverside views and a growing hospitality scene. It’s compact and walkable, with food and cultural experiences that appeal to first-time and repeat visitors.

- The south coast (Algarve): The south coast is a top pick for beach vibes. Warm weather, coastal towns and resort areas offer relaxed holiday energy. Even when crowds peak, many POC travellers note the overall atmosphere stays positive.

Practical tips for planning
- Prioritise Lisbon for arrival and orientation: international connections and city services make it an easy starting point. From Lisbon you can day-trip to nearby coastlines.
- Include Porto for a contrast in architecture, riverfront dining and a quieter pace relative to the capital.
- Book south coast stays if you want sun, beaches and relaxed holiday vibes—choose towns that suit your style: lively resorts or quieter fishing villages.

Expectations and vibes
- Many POC travellers report generally welcoming experiences in these areas. Vibes are described as upbeat across Lisbon, Porto and the south coast.
- As with any destination, individual experiences vary. Research neighbourhoods, accommodation reviews and local recommendations to match your comfort and interests.

Closing / Call to action
Use this short guide to shape your Portugal trip: start in Lisbon, add Porto, and reserve time for the south coast if you want beach days. Which spot fits your travel style—city culture, riverside charm, or coastal relaxation?

Join a relaxed afternoon stroll through Lisbon’s streets, cafes and viewpoints—authentic moments from a Lisbon travel vlog. #ditl #travel #vlog #lisbon
Spend the Afternoon with Me in Lisbon

Experience a relaxed Lisbon afternoon through street scenes, neighborhood cafés, and city viewpoints. This travel glimpse follows an easygoing pace—walking cobblestone alleys, stopping for coffee, and pausing at miradouros to take in views over the red roofs and the Tagus River.

Start in a central neighborhood, wandering narrow lanes lined with azulejo-tiled façades and small shops. The rhythm is pedestrian: a steady walk, occasional stops to photograph tiles or window displays, and short detours into local markets. Cafés provide natural pauses—order a bica or pastel de nata, sit at a small table, and watch passersby while soaking up the light.

Continue toward a popular viewpoint. Climb low flights of stairs or catch a short tram ride to reach a miradouro where the city unfolds below. Spend time framing shots of the rooftops, capturing the mix of historic buildings and river horizons. These moments highlight Lisbon’s blend of everyday life and scenic panoramas.

Include a casual meal at a neighborhood tasca or modern bistro—fresh seafood, simple plates, and local wine fit the mood of an unhurried afternoon. After eating, stroll along streets that slope toward the river, noticing tilework, street art, and the occasional Fado guitar floating from an open doorway.

End the afternoon with golden-hour light on the river or from a hilltop viewpoint. Reflect on the calm pace, the sensory details—coffee aroma, tiled patterns, river breeze—and invite viewers to imagine their own slow afternoon in Lisbon.

Call to action: Which Lisbon spot would you visit for an afternoon? Share your favorite cafés or viewpoints to inspire the next relaxed Lisbon itinerary.

Looking for the ultimate Lisbon experience? 🌟 Join me as I share my adventures in this breathtaking city, which I fell in love with even more during my visit in 2022! Here’s the perfect one-day itinerary to explore Lisbon's vibrant culture, delicious cuisine, and stunning architecture. 

Start your day at the iconic **Elevador de Bica** 🚃, where Lisbon's famous yellow trams make for a fantastic photo opportunity! Next, take a relaxing stroll along the serene **Green Street** 🍃—a hidden gem that offers a peaceful morning retreat. Don’t miss stopping by **Manteigaria**, where you can savor a **pasteis de nata** 🥐—the legendary custard tart made fresh right before your eyes!

Continue your adventure by passing through the magnificent **Arco de Rua Augusta**, which grants you breathtaking views of **Praça de Comércio** and the river 🌊. For lunch, head to the **Time Out Market** 🍽️, a bustling spot where you can indulge in a range of local dishes, from fresh seafood to traditional Portuguese snacks.

In the afternoon, explore the colorful **Pink Street** ☂️, a lively area that radiates energy and character. Make sure to visit the **Carmo Convent** 🙏, an open-air Gothic site featuring stunning architecture and a compelling museum.

As the sun sets, sip a drink with a view at **Seen Lisboa** 🌆, where you can unwind and take in the beauty of the city. Finally, wrap up your evening at **JNCQUOI Avenida** 🍴, a trendy restaurant known for its unique disco-themed facilities and live DJ on weekends.

Don't forget to save this itinerary for your upcoming Lisbon trip—I promise you won't regret it! Are you planning to visit Lisbon soon? Let me know!

description:
📌🇵🇹 Lisbon doesn’t have to be expensive  Here are 10 amazing things to do in Lisbon for under 10€ — perfect if you are traveling on a budget but will want the best experiences throughout the city ✨ Think viewpoints, local bites, and hidden gems most tourists overlook 💌 🔒Save this for your next trip to Lisbon and follow @martaplore 💘 for more budget travel guides #lisbontravel #thingstodoinlisbon #lisbonportugal #budgettraveltips #cheaptravel
Discover 10 incredible experiences in Lisbon for under 10€. Perfect for budget travelers seeking local gems! #lisbontravel #thingstodoinlisbon #budgettraveltips
Lisbon is a stunning city filled with charm, culture, and history—and it doesn't have to break the bank! With various activities and experiences that cost less than 10€, budget travelers can explore the best of what Lisbon has to offer without compromising on quality. Here are 10 amazing things to do in Lisbon that are wallet-friendly and will give you an authentic taste of the city.

1. **Visit Miradouros (Viewpoints)**: Lisbon is known for its breathtaking viewpoints, or 'miradouros', that offer stunning vistas of the city and the Tagus River. Places like Miradouro da Senhora do Monte are free to visit, allowing you to snap incredible photos of the skyline.

2. **Sample Local Bites at Mercado da Ribeira**: This iconic food market, also known as Time Out Market, features a range of local delights. While some dishes can be pricey, you can find affordable bites to sample, keeping it under 10€.

3. **Explore Alfama’s Narrow Streets**: Strolling through the historic Alfama district is a must. The labyrinth of narrow streets is free to explore and filled with colorful buildings, street art, and a vibrant atmosphere.

4. **Ride Tram 28**: Hop on the famous Tram 28 for a scenic ride through the city’s hills. A single ticket is typically under 3€, making it a great budget option to see key landmarks.

5. **Visit the Centro Cultural de Belém**: Offering a mix of exhibitions, concerts, and cultural events, many activities here are free or low-cost. Check their calendar for free entrance days.

6. **Take a Walk Along the Riverfront**: Lisbon's waterfront scenery is beautiful and accessible. Enjoy a serene walk along the Tagus River, taking in the sights of Praça do Comércio and the 25 de Abril Bridge.

7. **Enjoy Fado Music**: While many Fado venues can be costly, there are spots, particularly in Alfama, where you can hear live Fado music without spending too much. Be sure to ask locals for tips!

8. **Visit the National Museum of Ancient Art**: This museum offers a free entrance on specific days of the month, making it an excellent opportunity to explore Lisbon's art scene without spending much.

9. **Discover Jardim do Príncipe Real**: This picturesque garden is perfect for picnics and relaxation. Enjoy nature and local markets often held in the area, all at little to no cost.

10. **Relax at Praia de Carcavelos**: Just a short train ride from the city, this beautiful beach is free to visit. Spend a day soaking up the sun and enjoying the ocean waves—perfectly budget-friendly!

These activities are not only affordable but also offer a unique perspective of Lisbon that many tourists might overlook. Save this list for your next trip and don't forget to follow @martaplore for more budget travel guides!

description:
i was fighting for my life in lisbon fr #lisbon #lisbonportugal #travel #eurosummer #traveltiktok 
Real experiences navigating Lisbon's steep inclines and slippery cobblestones — practical tips to stay safe and enjoy the city. #lisbon #travel #lisbonportugal #eurosummer #traveltiktok
Lead
I was fighting for my life in Lisbon — literally struggling with the city’s insane inclines and slippery cobblestone streets. If you’ve heard “just travel when you’re older,” this account explains why Lisbon’s terrain can feel impossible and offers practical ways to stay safe and enjoy the city now.

Why Lisbon’s streets are challenging
Lisbon is built on seven hills. Narrow lanes, steep inclines and traditional cobblestone pavements (calçada) create a beautiful but demanding walking environment. The combination of slope, uneven stones and occasional wet surfaces can make basic walking feel unstable, especially after rain or when carrying luggage.

What to expect on the ground
- Long, steep climbs between neighborhoods (e.g., Alfama, Bairro Alto).  
- Irregular, rounded cobbles that can shift underfoot or become slippery.  
- Frequent stairways and uneven curbs instead of smooth sidewalks.  
- Crowded tourist areas where jostling increases slip risk.

Practical tips to stay safe and comfortable
- Wear the right shoes: choose sturdy, closed-toe footwear with grippy soles and good ankle support. Avoid smooth-soled dress shoes or flip-flops.  
- Use walking aids when needed: a small collapsible walking pole or sturdy backpack straps can help with balance on steep stretches.  
- Pace yourself: take breaks, sit at viewpoints, and plan shorter walking segments between transit stops.  
- Choose routes carefully: use tram 28 or elevators/ascensores where possible to avoid the steepest climbs. Look for streets with flatter gradients.  
- Watch the weather: cobblestones are far more slippery when wet — delay long walking plans in rain.  
- Travel light: heavy bags throw off your center of gravity on inclines and stairs. Use luggage with wheels for transfers and a small daypack for exploring.  
- Mind your step: watch for uneven stones, sudden steps and drainage grates. Take deliberate steps rather than hurried strides.

Practical packing checklist
- Comfortable walking shoes with traction  
- Lightweight daypack and luggage with wheels  
- Small first-aid kit (bandages, blister pads)  
- Reusable water bottle to stay hydrated on climbs  
- Lightweight rain jacket and a small umbrella for sudden showers

When to ask for help or change plans
If inclines cause breathlessness, dizziness, or repeated falls, stop and seek help. Consider changing plans to use more public transit, take taxis for hilly transfers, or book accommodations in lower-elevation neighborhoods to minimize daily climbs.

Closing takeaway
Lisbon’s hills and cobblestones are part of its charm, but they can turn a casual walk into a struggle. With the right shoes, sensible route choices, and realistic pacing, you can enjoy Lisbon safely now — no need to “just travel when you’re older.” What part of Lisbon worries you most when planning a visit?

description:
How we spent our first day in Lisbon 🫶🏼🚋✨🇵🇹 #lisbon #portugal #lisboa #travelvlog #travelwithme 
Discover how we spent our first perfect day in Lisbon, from sunrise views to delicious local cuisine! #lisbon #portugal #travelvlog
Lisbon, the stunning capital of Portugal, offers a vibrant mix of history, charm, and culinary delights. On our first day in this beautiful city, we started bright and early, landing at 6:00 AM local time after our flight from Miami. The sunrise over the city was breathtaking and set the tone for an exciting day ahead.

After checking into one of the most beautiful boutique hotels I have ever stayed in, we were treated to a delightful breakfast featuring fresh juices, croissants, homemade baked goods, and the best brownie bites I've ever tasted. Energized from our meal, we set out to explore the city.

Lisbon's charm is undeniable. As we walked through the lively streets, we stumbled upon an outdoor market and a quaint bookstore, which added to the local charm we were beginning to fall in love with. Our first culinary adventure included trying the famous pastel de nata. This delicious pastry, filled with a creamy custard and encased in flaky pastry, was simply perfect.

Our journey continued as we enjoyed the high-quality coffee from various cafes, each serving delightful iced lattes. The main pedestrian street led us straight to the impressive Augusta Arch, where we discovered a craft market bustling with talented vendors. I couldn't resist purchasing fish bag charms as mementos for my family.

As we continued our exploration, we took in the beauty of Lisbon’s main square and the stunning views of the water, all while navigating the city’s steep hills. One of the highlights was visiting the Lisbon Cathedral, an architectural masterpiece complemented by the iconic yellow trams that ran right in front of it.

With our appetites growing, we found a charming restaurant that served Portuguese small plates. The fusion of flavors, including a Brazilian flare, made our tapas-style dining experience truly memorable. 

After a long day of walking and exploring, we returned to our hotel to unwind. Our room was immaculate, spacious, and featured an incredible view from the balcony – a perfect spot to soak in the beauty of Lisbon.

For dinner, we headed to the renowned Time Out Market, where countless food stalls offered an enticing array of dishes to satisfy every craving. After indulging in a feast, we fell asleep content, reflecting on our unforgettable first day in this enchanting city of Lisbon. Future adventures await as we enjoy more of what Portugal has to offer!

A few things to highlight :  Transport : We caught Ubers & tram, Ubers are super cheap in Lisbon so we preferred doing that but also some places are very walkable  Restaurants : make sure to book any before travelling as some are booked out. We had to wait 1.5 hours for Ponte final.  Cafes : Most are walk ins  Activities: Make sure to book any palaces in sintra in advance as it gets busy.  Day 1 : explore chiado, shopping street & the plaza. As well as cafe hopping & trying many pastel de natas. I recommend grabbing brunch at Heim, Rhodo, dear breakfast. Also check out the tram 28 area to see the famous tram.  Day 2 : We decided on doing our day trip on the 2nd day, we took an uber from Etma bakery to Quinta de Regaleria, you can catch the train however the uber was cheap so we decided to save time by doing so. We only spent 2 hours in Quinta however I would’ve stayed longer had we not had timed tickets to pena. So definitely consider spending longer between the both. It was also another 20 min uber to get to pena. Then we went to pena & spent a couple of hours exploring, pena was abit underwhelming in comparison to Quinta. After that we headed back to Lisbon & checked out the time out markets whilst waiting for our res at Lupita. This place is ranked top 20 in Europe & it did not disappoint. There was a 1.5 hour wait for it though.  Day 3 : We spent the day exploring alfama, the cathedral and Saturday markets. Brunch at rhodo which was AMAZING, after we finished exploring we headed to MAAT museum and checked it out. Then went back and strolled around the city before heading to Zubir chicken for one last feed. The best chicken ever.  Enjoy!  #lisbon #lisbonfoodguide #travellisboa #travelvlog #lisbonthingstodo

description:
how to visit 3 neighborhoods in Lisbon without rushing: Rua da Silva, Principe Real and Bairo Alto/Chiado #lisbonportugal #travelitinerary #lisbontravel #travelguide #portugaltravel mapped 4 days in Lisbon what to do in Lisbon perfect day in Lisbon what to do in chiado Lisbon 
A relaxed one-day route to visit Rua da Silva, Príncipe Real and Bairro Alto/Chiado without rushing — mapped stops, highlights and timing. #lisbonportugal #travelitinerary #lisbontravel
Plan a relaxed day to visit three distinct Lisbon neighborhoods — Rua da Silva, Príncipe Real and Bairro Alto/Chiado — without rushing. This route groups nearby highlights and realistic timing so you can enjoy each area.

Start: Rua da Silva
Lead off in Rua da Silva to see a local, less-touristy street vibe. Walk slowly along the street, note cafés, small shops and everyday Lisbon life. Allow 60–90 minutes for coffee, window shopping and photos.

Next: Príncipe Real
From Rua da Silva head to Príncipe Real, known for gardens, boutique shops and viewpoints. Spend 90–120 minutes here: visit a miradouro, stroll the Jardim do Príncipe Real, browse design stores and enjoy a casual lunch at a nearby café.

Finish: Bairro Alto and Chiado
End the day in Bairro Alto and Chiado for classic Lisbon architecture, bookstores, viewpoints and nightlife-ready streets. Allocate 90–120 minutes to wander Chiado’s squares, pop into historic cafés, explore narrow Bairro Alto lanes and catch sunset views.

Timing and tips
- Total relaxed time: roughly 4–5 hours, adjustable to a full mapped day across four days in Lisbon.
- Move on foot between close points; use short tram or taxi rides if needed to save energy.
- Prioritize one sit-down meal and two coffee stops to keep the pace unhurried.

Takeaway
This mapped, unhurried route lets you experience Rua da Silva’s local character, Príncipe Real’s green and boutique scene, and Bairro Alto/Chiado’s historic heart in a single relaxed outing. Which neighborhood do you want to linger in longer on your next Lisbon visit?

### A Perfect One-Day Itinerary in Lisbon 🇵🇹

If you're planning to spend just one day in the beautiful city of Lisbon, you're in for a treat! While I always recommend taking more time to explore, Lisbon is quite easy to navigate, making it possible to pack a full day of adventure into your itinerary. Here’s how to make the most of your day in this stunning city.

**Morning:**  
Start your day in **Alfama**, the oldest neighborhood in Lisbon, and if you're visiting on a Tuesday or Saturday, don't miss the vibrant flea market! After exploring, take a leisurely stroll to **Miradouro das Portas do Sol** and **Miradouro Santa Luzia**. These viewpoints offer breathtaking views over the city and are just a short walk from one another. ☀️

Next, grab a coffee at **Café de Garagem**, known for its incredible vistas. It's the perfect way to energize before continuing your adventure. Then, hop onto the iconic **Tram 28**. Riding one of these historic yellow trams is a must when in Lisbon. You can stay on for as long or as little as you wish, allowing for flexibility in your plans.

**Midday:**  
Disembark at **Praça do Comércio**, a grand plaza that overlooks the Tagus River. From there, make your way to **Pink Street**, a lively area filled with vibrant bars and restaurants. Don't forget to stop by the **Time Out Market** to indulge in a delicious **pastel de nata** – a quintessential Portuguese treat! 🥐

For lunch, enjoy a meal with a view at **Java Rooftop**, where you'll savor delicious cuisine surrounded by the cityscape. Afterward, take the **Bica Funicular** to reach the trendy **Bairro Alto** neighborhood.

**Afternoon:**  
From Bairro Alto, take a taxi to **Belém**, known for its historical significance and monuments. A quick taxi ride will also lead you to the **LX Factory**, a unique cultural space filled with eclectic shops, restaurants, and creative spaces. It’s the perfect spot to explore and perhaps pick up a souvenir! 🎨

**Evening:**  
As the day winds down, head over to **Jardim do Duque** for a spectacular sunset view. This beautiful garden provides a serene atmosphere for relaxation. Finally, treat yourself to dinner at **Ponto Final**, renowned for its stunning views and delectable dishes. 🌅

With this itinerary, you can experience the essence of Lisbon in just one day! For more inspiration and details, check out my blog.

Lead
Most POC travellers visiting Portugal single out three go-to areas: Lisbon, Porto and the south coast. These spots consistently come up in recommendations for atmosphere, accessibility and welcoming vibes.

What POC travellers report about each place
- Lisbon: The capital is the most commonly chosen destination. It offers diverse neighbourhoods, a vibrant cultural scene, frequent international flights, and visible communities that make navigating city life easier. Expect plentiful cafés, museums, nightlife and easy public transport.

- Porto: Portugal’s second city is the next most-ordered pick. Porto combines historic streets, riverside views and a growing hospitality scene. It’s compact and walkable, with food and cultural experiences that appeal to first-time and repeat visitors.

- The south coast (Algarve): The south coast is a top pick for beach vibes. Warm weather, coastal towns and resort areas offer relaxed holiday energy. Even when crowds peak, many POC travellers note the overall atmosphere stays positive.

Practical tips for planning
- Prioritise Lisbon for arrival and orientation: international connections and city services make it an easy starting point. From Lisbon you can day-trip to nearby coastlines.
- Include Porto for a contrast in architecture, riverfront dining and a quieter pace relative to the capital.
- Book south coast stays if you want sun, beaches and relaxed holiday vibes—choose towns that suit your style: lively resorts or quieter fishing villages.

Expectations and vibes
- Many POC travellers report generally welcoming experiences in these areas. Vibes are described as upbeat across Lisbon, Porto and the south coast.
- As with any destination, individual experiences vary. Research neighbourhoods, accommodation reviews and local recommendations to match your comfort and interests.

Closing / Call to action
Use this short guide to shape your Portugal trip: start in Lisbon, add Porto, and reserve time for the south coast if you want beach days. Which spot fits your travel style—city culture, riverside charm, or coastal relaxation?

Follow a relaxed Day 1 in Alfama: São Jorge Castle, pastel de nata at António, Miradouro de Santa Luzia, Feira da Ladra, lunch in Alfama and sunset at the miradouro. #lisbonportugal #travelguide
Lead
A relaxed first day in Lisbon focused on Alfama gives you the feel of the city without turning sightseeing into a checklist. This guide follows the video’s route: start at São Jorge Castle, enjoy local pastry and coffee, visit two miradouros (viewpoints), browse Feira da Ladra when available, wander Alfama’s streets, and finish with dinner and sunset near the cathedral.

Morning — São Jorge Castle and a pastel de nata
Begin at São Jorge Castle to take in sweeping views over Lisbon. From the castle, stroll down a street filled with local shops and stop at António for your first pastel de nata. The video highlights António’s version as “not too sweet” — pair it with a cup of coffee and take your time to soak in the neighborhood.

Mid-morning — Miradouros and photos
Walk toward the riverside and visit Miradouro de Santa Luzia and Miradouro das Portas do Sol. Both are iconic viewpoints in Alfama. They can be busier later in the day, but photos taken now are less likely to be backlogged. Enjoy the tile-lined benches, rooftops, and river views.

Optional detour — Feira da Ladra (Tuesdays and Saturdays)
If your visit falls on a Tuesday or Saturday, make a quick detour to Feira da Ladra, Lisbon’s flea market. The video recommends it for cool trinkets and an indoor market with goods from local artisans. It’s a short walk from the miradouros and worth a browse before continuing your route.

Lunchtime — Wander Alfama’s alleys
Return to Alfama’s middle section and drift through tight streets and alleyways. This area is ideal for lunch; the video mentions Casa São Miguel as a whimsical spot (note: the pastel there wasn’t tried in the clip). Choose a small local restaurant to sample traditional dishes and continue enjoying the neighborhood atmosphere.

Afternoon — Drift toward Praça do Comércio
After lunch, the suggested plan is to drift from Alfama down toward Praça do Comércio. Let the streets guide you rather than forcing a strict schedule — the point is a relaxed exploration that captures Alfama’s character.

Evening — Dinner by the cathedral and sunset
Have dinner near Lisbon Cathedral. Afterward, head back up to one of the miradouros for an iconic sunset over the city and river. This finishes Day 1 on a classic Lisbon note: food, views, and historic streets.

Close / Takeaway
This Day 1 itinerary emphasizes a slow, enjoyable pace in Alfama: São Jorge Castle, pastel de nata at António, Santa Luzia and Portas do Sol viewpoints, Feira da Ladra on market days, wandering for lunch, and a sunset by the miradouro after dinner near the cathedral. Does this relaxed route match how you like to explore cities, or would you add specific museums or tram rides?

Discover how to experience the vibrant city of Lisbon in just three days with this curated itinerary, designed to help you make the most of your trip.

**Day 1: Historic Charm and Sunset Views**
Begin your Lisbon adventure by immersing yourself in the historic Alfama district. Allow yourself to wander through its narrow streets and discover hidden gems. Visit the Convento do Carmo, a captivating ruin that is sure to leave a lasting impression. Experience the iconic Tram 28, a quintessential Lisbon experience that, despite its popularity with tourists, remains a worthwhile journey. Conclude your day with a breathtaking sunset at Miradouro de Santa Luzia; arriving a bit early is recommended to secure a good spot.

**Day 2: Castles, Pastries, and Riverside Serenity**
Start your second day at Castelo de São Jorge in the morning, before the crowds arrive. No trip to Lisbon is complete without indulging in Pastéis de Belém; queue up, order two, and savor them warm outside. Afterward, head to the nearby Belém Tower, a magnificent landmark. For a truly memorable experience, take a sunset boat cruise on the Tagus River – it's considered one of the best activities in Lisbon.

**Day 3: Local Flavors and Creative Hubs**
On your final day, start your morning with coffee at Café São Miguel, opting for local spots over tourist traps. Explore LX Factory, a trendy industrial complex, especially vibrant on Sundays. Inside, discover Ler Devagar, a unique bookstore housed within an old factory, offering a truly cool atmosphere. Enjoy brunch at Leonetta before finishing your day at Icon Bar Rooftop for drinks with panoramic views of the entire city.

**Unforgettable Lisbon Experiences:**
Don't miss out on savoring a fresh, warm pastel de nata. Witnessing a sunset from a miradouro offers unparalleled beauty. Getting intentionally lost in Alfama, with no particular destination in mind, allows for authentic discovery. Three days may pass quickly, but Lisbon's charm will undoubtedly stay with you. Beat the heat in Lisbon with seven cool activities—from shaded walks to seaside escapes—plus practical tips to stay comfortable. #lisbon #lisboa #thingstodo #heatwave #tourism
Lisbon in a heat wave calls for planning activities that keep comfort and enjoyment balanced. These seven suggestions prioritize shade, water, and cooler hours while making the most of the city.

1. Morning Alfama or Mouraria walks in shade
Start early with narrow streets in Alfama or Mouraria. The maze-like alleys provide shade, historic views, and less crowding before temperatures peak.

2. Visit museums and indoor attractions midday
Use the hottest hours to visit indoor sites—museums, churches, and historic buildings—where cooler indoor air and exhibits offer relief from the sun.

3. Take a riverside stroll along the Tagus
The riverfront by Praça do Comércio and Cais do Sodré stays cooler with breezes. Walk, sit at a terrace, or enjoy the view while staying near water.

4. Ferry or boat ride to nearby beaches and towns
Short ferries across the Tagus or quick boat trips open up beach options and coastal breezes—ideal for escaping city heat without long travel.

5. Afternoon at Lisbon beaches or Cascais
Head to nearby beaches like Costa da Caparica or Cascais in the afternoon for sea swims, seaside cafés, and cooling winds.

6. Cool down with local gelato, cafés, or shaded patios
Seek gelato shops, iced drinks, and cafés with shaded patios. Small stops for refreshments prolong comfort during sightseeing.

7. Evening miradouros and Alfresco dining after sunset
Plan sightseeing for late afternoon and evening: miradouros (viewpoints) cool off, and open-air dining offers a pleasant end to hot days.

Practical tips
- Stay hydrated and carry a refillable bottle.
- Wear breathable clothing, a hat, and sunscreen.
- Time outdoor activities for early morning or late afternoon.
- Use public transport or short ferries to reduce walking in direct sun.

Takeaway
Lisbon remains enjoyable during a heat wave when you prioritize shade, water, and timing. Choose early walks, indoor cultural stops at midday, and coastal escapes or evening viewpoints to make hot days comfortable. Which of these will you try first on your trip to Lisboa?

One of the coolest places to visit in Lisbon! 📸✨🏭 📍 Once an old industrial complex for textiles, the @LXFactory has transformed into a vibrant, artistic village nestled within the city. Here, abandoned warehouses are now cafés, restaurants, concept stores and more! 🧺 You should visit on a Sunday, when there is a weekly flea market with dozens of temporary booths and stalls, where you can find locally-made jewelry, vintage clothes, cork-crafted items, plants and more. 🎨 Do not hesitate to go inside the big industrial building where you will find many boutiques, concept stores, art studios and creative company offices. 📚 Lastly, check out the Ler Devagar bookstore! @LerDevagar, meaning “Read Slowly” is more than a bookstore; it is also a cultural space for events, temporary and permanent exhibitions. It was actually named one of the top ten most beautiful bookstores in the world! Planning on visiting Lisbon soon? Save this reel and follow @thetravellingfrenchy for the best tips 🫶🏼   #lxfactory #lisbon #lisbontravel #lisbonportugal🇵🇹 #lisbonguide     Unique experiences in Lisbon • Things to do in Lisbon • Unconventional Lisbon adventures • Hidden gems in Lisbon • Unusual Lisbon • Off-the-beaten-path Lisbon
Discover LX Factory Lisbon — transformed warehouses with cafés, boutiques, a Sunday flea market, and the famous Ler Devagar bookstore. Tips for visiting and what to see. #lxfactory #lisbon #lisbontravel
Lead
LX Factory in Lisbon is a repurposed industrial complex turned creative village — one of the city's coolest places to visit. Once textile warehouses, the area now houses cafés, restaurants, concept stores, art studios, creative offices and a weekly flea market that draws locals and travelers alike.

What LX Factory offers
- Transformed industrial buildings: Large factory halls and loft-like spaces converted into shops, studios and eateries. The industrial architecture and street art create a distinctly urban, artistic atmosphere.
- Cafés and restaurants: A mix of casual cafés and inventive restaurants occupying former warehouse spaces, ideal for coffee, brunch or a relaxed meal between browsing stores.
- Concept stores and boutiques: Independent shops selling locally-made jewelry, cork-crafted items, design objects and unique homeware.
- Art studios and creative offices: Galleries, studios and small creative companies open to the public or visible from communal areas.
- Ler Devagar bookstore: A standout cultural space named “Read Slowly.” More than a bookstore, it hosts events, exhibitions and was listed among the world’s most beautiful bookstores.

Sunday flea market (best time to visit)
- When to go: Sundays are recommended for the weekly flea market. Numerous temporary booths and stalls appear across the complex.
- What to find: Vintage clothes, locally-made jewelry, cork products, plants and other handmade or secondhand items — great for souvenirs and discovering local makers.

Practical tips for visitors
- Timing: Visit on Sunday for the full market experience; weekdays are quieter but still great for cafés and shops.
- Explore inside big industrial buildings: Many boutiques, concept stores and studios are housed inside the larger structures — don’t just stick to the outer courtyards.
- Plan for photos: The combination of industrial architecture and street art makes LX Factory a photogenic spot — bring a camera.
- Combine with a Lisbon itinerary: LX Factory fits well into an afternoon exploring alternative Lisbon neighborhoods and cultural sites.

Why LX Factory is worth it

Lisbon, Portugal's picturesque capital, is filled with vibrant culture, stunning architecture, and delectable cuisine. Whether you're a first-time visitor or returning to explore its hidden gems, here are eight must-do activities for an unforgettable experience in this charming city.

1. **Stroll Down Pink Street**: A popular nightlife hub, Pink Street is known for its lively atmosphere and vibrant bars. Perfect for enjoying a pre-dinner drink while soaking in the city's energy.

2. **Ride the Elevador da Bica**: This iconic funicular offers breathtaking views of the narrow streets below. A must-ride experience that combines stunning scenery with a taste of Lisbon's historic charm.

3. **Visit LX Factory**: This former industrial site has transformed into a creative space filled with shops, restaurants, and art galleries. Make sure to explore the unique boutiques and enjoy a meal at one of the trendy eateries.

4. **Discover the Padrāo dos Descobrimentos**: This impressive monument celebrates the Age of Discoveries and provides insight into Portugal’s maritime heritage. Climb to the top for fantastic views of the Tagus River.

5. **Explore the Green Street**: This charming area is perfect for a relaxing stroll. Enjoy the greenery and locally inspired artwork scattered around this peaceful section of the city.

6. **Taste Pastéis de Belém (Custard Tart)**: No trip to Lisbon is complete without indulging in a famous pastéis de nata from Pastéis de Belém. This creamy custard tart is a true Portuguese delight!

7. **Walk the Beautiful Streets**: Lisbon’s streets are lined with beautiful tiles and captivating street art. Take time to wander and capture the essence of the city’s stunning architecture.

8. **Relax at Empire Square Garden**: Finally, unwind at this serene garden. Enjoy the lush landscape and take a moment to appreciate the tranquil setting amidst the bustling city life.

These activities will immerse you in the heart of Lisbon and leave you with lasting memories. Prepare for an incredible adventure filled with culture, history, and delicious flavors!

🇵🇹👇 This list is for you if you're visiting Lisbon for the first time: ✨ Praça do Comércio – Start your day at Lisbon’s most iconic (and touristic) square by the river. ✨ Lisbon Cathedral – Walk uphill to the oldest cathedral in the city, also known as Sé de Lisboa. ✨ Miradouro de Santa Luzia – One of the most beautiful viewpoints, with azulejos (tiles) and views over the Alfama neighborhood. ✨ History of Lisbon Mural by Nuno Saraiva – A colorful mural that tells Lisbon’s history through illustrations. ✨ Explore Alfama – Walk down the stairs and get lost in Lisbon’s oldest neighborhood. ✨ Casa São Miguel cafe– A charming bakery in Alfama famous for its traditional Portuguese pastries ✨ Ginjinha da Dona Alice – Stop for a quick shot of Lisbon’s famous cherry liqueur. ✨ Taberna Sal Grosso – Perfect spot for a  Portuguese lunch (book in advance). ✨ National Pantheon – An iconic monument with panoramic views from the terrace. ✨ Feira da Ladra – Lisbon’s famous flea market (Tuesdays & Saturdays). ✨ Igreja de São Vicente de Fora – A stunning church with one of the best rooftop views in Lisbon. ✨ Miradouro da Graça – A popular viewpoint with beautiful city and river views, perfect for photos or a quick coffee outside. #lisbontravel #lisbonportugal #lisboaportugal 
Discover Lisbon's must-see spots for first-time travelers! From iconic squares to delightful pastries, explore the best of Portugal's capital. #lisbontravel #lisbonportugal #lisboaportugal
Planning your first trip to Lisbon? This beautiful city offers a wealth of attractions, perfect for first-time visitors. Start your adventure at Praça do Comércio, Lisbon’s most iconic square, where you can soak up the vibrant atmosphere by the river. From here, take a stroll to the Lisbon Cathedral, known as Sé de Lisboa, the oldest cathedral in the city that showcases stunning architecture and historical significance.

Don’t miss out on the Miradouro de Santa Luzia, one of the most picturesque viewpoints in Lisbon. Adorned with beautiful azulejos (traditional tiles), it provides breathtaking views of the Alfama neighborhood. While in the area, take note of the History of Lisbon Mural by Nuno Saraiva. This colorful work of art narrates the story of Lisbon through engaging illustrations.

Exploring Alfama is an essential experience; wander through its narrow streets, and embrace the charm of the city’s oldest neighborhood. Make sure to stop by Casa São Miguel cafe for traditional Portuguese pastries, a perfect treat during your adventure.

For a taste of local culture, try Ginjinha da Dona Alice, renowned for its delightful cherry liqueur. For lunch, Taberna Sal Grosso offers a delightful selection of Portuguese dishes—just be sure to book in advance to secure your spot.

Continue your exploration at the National Pantheon, an iconic monument that provides panoramic views of the city from its terrace, an experience not to be missed. If you love shopping for unique finds, the Feira da Ladra, Lisbon’s famous flea market, is open on Tuesdays and Saturdays, making it a great place to pick up souvenirs.

Lastly, visit Igreja de São Vicente de Fora, a stunning church that boasts one of the best rooftop views of Lisbon. For another great vantage point, head to the Miradouro da Graça, popular for its beautiful city and river views. Whether you are taking photos or enjoying a coffee, this viewpoint is a lovely spot to unwind.

These attractions are just a glimpse of what Lisbon has to offer. Be sure to immerse yourself in the culture, enjoy local delicacies, and embrace the vibrant atmosphere of Portugal’s captivating capital.
